solutions.solution: It is invalid; the speech may have been made by anybody. It is not implicit. The politician says that people of all castes were worried; this makes the belief that his belief in casteism. He may believe in casteism or he may also, of transcending the barriers of caste and religion.

It is obviously implicit. If corruption is being talked about it must be existing. It is implicit. It is slightly tricky. The politician may or may not be corrupt. As a fact, I may be true or false, but this is a question of assumption. For this we need to consider who is making the statement. Please note carefully that the politician himself is making this statement against corruption. While making this statement he obviously assumes that he himself is honest because this is the image that he wants to project. Hence It is valid. It is implicit in the statement.
It is a restatement. If mind be assumed otherwise why would the captain want to stop sailing? III is again not certain, an island may or may not be near.
Since a pale look implies the possibility of the disease, assumption II is valid. Others are not necessarily true.
The tone of the statement implies II. I is not mentioned: 'morality' is not the theme. III is invalid because the statement is just an exclamation, not a prayer.

The woman asks God to help her / her intentions have been pure. But this does not imply that God would not have helped her if her intentions were not pure. Hence, I is invalid. Similarly, II is also invalid. III is a valid assumption - when somebody prays, she must be assuming (taking for granted) that God answers prayers.

The theme of the passage is that learning from mistakes is important; mistakes themselves are not important. Hence, I is implicit. Again, the second sentence says that learning from mistakes makes great men while the third sentence says that because of it, there are very few great men. This naturally implies that very few learn from mistakes. Hence, II is implicit. III is not an assumption, it is a restatement.

It is only said that the name was misspelt and that it is regretted. It would not be proper to draw conclusions such as I or II. Only III is implicit. If it were not desirable, the name would be spelt correctly.

The singer of the politician is for the fact that the government is talking of soft drinks when drinking water is not available. This justifies assumption II. I and III relate the politician's comment to health considerations. But this is not true. The politician is talking of priorities only.

These both mention 'popularity' of some songs; the second statement mentions 'buying these cassettes'. The way these two are related in the statement, it becomes obvious that the writer assumes that buying.

I talks of some details. But the statement has not given these details. How do we know what exactly was there in ISRO case, on the basis of the given statement alone? So, I is not implicit. II is clear. Even if it is true, the statement only talks of what is unexpected, it could not have shocked. III is obviously implicit.
It is a restatement of and III are obvious.

The tone of the statement implies that I and III are both implicit in the statement. The person links political connections - blindly, so to say - with corruption. Therefore, I and III are implicit. II is not implicit. It may be true in general, but, in the person's statement, it is never implied.

The analyst talks of hungry stomachs, so people with hungry stomachs must be existing. It means that I is implicit. It is NOT implicit although it looks otherwise. Some rice must have been available because of people could not have been hungry. But rice would have been available only in limited amount (otherwise people wouldn't have been hungry). Hence, III is implicit.

It is not implicit; no clue is given about the exact identity of Sanba. It is implicit; it is clear from the use of the word 'myth'. The statement says that Indian army's discipline was believed in, although, it was not true and that it was a 'myth'. Myth or otherwise, such belief did exist. III is implicit in the tone of the statement: look at the way the statement links a 'spying case' with non-existence of 'discipline'. It is a derogatory fashion in which these two are linked. This implies III.

The way the phrase 'easiest-to-use' is highlighted in the advertisement, it is apparent that people look for convenience in a cellular phone. Hence, I is implicit. But it is not clear whether the size of the phone is also highlighted. It may be of the tone: 'Small in size, but huge in performance' or it may be like: 'Not only small in size, but also huge in performance.' In case one, the advertiser is apologetic about the size; in the other, he is boastful of it. So it could be either of these situations and hence It is not implicit. III is not hinted in the statement.

It is obviously implicit: What is being suggested must be desirable. It may not be true. The brother talks of 'greater' transparency. It means that some degree of transparency was already there and 'greater' transparency
